name: Gross Margin
description: Gross profit and gross margin for a unit or order line: profit is selling price minus unit cost, and margin expresses that profit as a percentage of the selling price (not of cost — that is markup).

inputs.name: Selling price
inputs.description: Net selling price per unit, after any discounts.

inputs.name: Unit cost
inputs.description: Cost of goods per unit — ideally the landed cost, not just the purchase price.

outputs.name: Gross margin
outputs.description: Profit as a percentage of the selling price.

expression: profit = P - C; margin_pct = 100 x (P - C) / P
assumptions: Price and cost refer to the same unit and the same currency.
Unit cost includes everything counted as cost of goods for the item (ideally landed cost).
Margin is expressed on price; markup on cost is a different, larger percentage for the same profit.
limitations: Requires a selling price greater than zero — margin is undefined at P = 0.
A per-unit gross view: it ignores overheads, payment fees, returns, and volume effects.
A negative result simply means the item sells below cost.

examples.note: Inverts the corpus's own margin-pricing example: a US$25 cost priced for a 10% margin via cost/(1 - margin rate) = 25/0.9 = US$27.78. Checking that price back through this formula returns profit 2.78 and a margin of about 10%.

citation.quote: if the current cost is US$25 and a 10 % margin is desired, the price is calculated by using the formula: cost/(1 - margin rate), or US$25/.9 = US$27.78

citations.quote: In margin pricing the buyer establishes a price that includes a predetermined profit margin. For example, if the current cost is US$25 and a 10 % margin is desired, the price is calculated by using the formula: cost/(1 - margin rate), or US$25/.9 = US$27.78.
